intro:
Ephraim was the younger son of Joseph by his Egyptian wife Asenath, born in Egypt before the famine. The name (“double fruitfulness”) voiced Joseph’s thanksgiving that “God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction” (Genesis 41:52). On his deathbed Jacob adopted Joseph’s two sons as his own and blessed them, deliberately crossing his hands to set his right hand on Ephraim the younger over Manasseh the firstborn, foretelling that the younger would be the greater (Genesis 48:14; Genesis 48:19) — one more instance of God’s pattern of choosing the younger throughout Genesis.

The tribe descended from Ephraim grew into one of the largest and most influential in Israel. Its territory lay in the fertile central hill country, with Shechem as a chief city and the tabernacle resting for centuries at Shiloh within its borders (Joshua 16:5; Joshua 18:1). Joshua son of Nun, who led the conquest, was an Ephraimite (Numbers 13:8). Proud of its standing, the tribe repeatedly contended for pre-eminence — quarrelling with Gideon and going to war with Jephthah (Judges 8:1; Judges 12:1-6).

After the kingdom split, Ephraim so dominated the northern tribes that the prophets regularly call the whole northern kingdom “Ephraim.” Its first king, Jeroboam, was an Ephraimite, and the name becomes shorthand for the North in Hosea, Isaiah, and Jeremiah — often in oracles of rebuke for idolatry: “Ephraim is joined to idols; leave him alone” (Hosea 4:17). Yet the same prophets voice the LORD’s aching love for wayward Ephraim: “How can I give you up, O Ephraim?… My compassion grows warm and tender” (Hosea 11:8), and “Ephraim is my firstborn” (Jeremiah 31:9).

The northern kingdom fell to Assyria in 722 BC, but the prophetic hope reached beyond the ruin to a future reunion: Ezekiel was told to join two sticks, one for Judah and one for “Joseph (the stick of Ephraim),” into one in the LORD’s hand, picturing a reunited people under one shepherd-king from David’s line (Ezekiel 37:16-19; Ezekiel 37:24). Ephraim thus stands both as a sobering portrait of covenant unfaithfulness and as an object of God’s persevering mercy in the restoration of all Israel.
